quotes.verbatim_quote: Just as the blues form is part of our "stock and trade," rhythm changes are a very important part of our vocabulary. This chord progression was originally found in the song I've Got Rhythm by George Gershwin. It has been the basis for literally dozens of songs, and has been a favorite progression of improvisers for decades.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines rhythm changes as foundational jazz concept with origin.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Oleo, by Sonny Rollins, Anthropology by Charlie Parker and The Flintstones would be examples of songs based on these changes. There are actually many variations on this progression, but the example below represents its most basic form.

quotes.topic: Soloing over Rhythm Changes — scale usage
quotes.verbatim_quote: This solo utilizes scales that you learned in both this book and Beginning Jazz Guitar. As you progress, you'll find that rhythm changes are usually highly altered and extended, so that more interesting scales and improvisational devices can be used. For now, just become familiar with the sound of this progression.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pedagogy: start with familiar scales, internalize the sound before alterations.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Use the CD that is available with this book, or record yourself playing the changes, so that you can practice playing along and improvising your own solo.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes play-along practice for developing rhythm-changes improvisation.
